Guideline: Define schema with gql tag and SDL
Description: Use GraphQL Schema Definition Language (SDL) with the gql tagged template literal. Define types, queries, and mutations clearly with descriptions and proper nullability.
Do: Use gql tag for type definitions; add descriptions to types and fields; be explicit about nullability
Dont: Do not define schemas as raw strings; do not make everything nullable by default; do not skip field descriptions
Code Good:
import { gql } from 'graphql-tag';

export const typeDefs = gql`
  """
  A registered user in the system.
  """
  type User {
    id: ID!
    email: String!
    name: String!
    avatar: String
    role: UserRole!
    posts(first: Int = 10, after: String): PostConnection!
    createdAt: DateTime!
  }

  enum UserRole {
    ADMIN
    EDITOR
    VIEWER
  }

  type Query {
    """
    Get the currently authenticated user.
    """
    me: User

    """
    Find a user by their ID.
    """
    user(id: ID!): User

    """
    List all users with pagination.
    """
    users(first: Int = 20, after: String): UserConnection!
  }

  type Mutation {
    updateProfile(input: UpdateProfileInput!): User!
    deleteAccount: Boolean!
  }
`;

Guideline: Organize resolvers by type with Query, Mutation, and field resolvers
Description: Structure resolvers as an object matching the schema types. Separate Query, Mutation, and field resolvers. Keep resolver functions thin by delegating to service layers.
Do: Separate resolvers by type; delegate business logic to service layers; keep resolvers thin
Dont: Do not put all logic in resolvers; do not mix database queries directly in resolver functions

Guideline: Configure ApolloServer with proper plugins and error handling
Description: Set up ApolloServer with type-safe resolvers, proper plugins for logging and shutdown, and production-ready configuration including introspection control.
Do: Use ApolloServer with plugins for logging and graceful shutdown; disable introspection in production
Dont: Do not use ApolloServer without plugins; do not enable introspection in production; do not skip graceful shutdown

Guideline: Configure GraphQL Yoga with proper plugins and envelop integration
Description: GraphQL Yoga is a batteries-included GraphQL server. Use createYoga() with envelop plugins for auth, validation, and other cross-cutting concerns.
Do: Use createYoga with envelop plugins; configure proper CORS and error masking; use schema from SDL or code-first
Dont: Do not use Yoga without error masking in production; do not skip plugin integration for cross-cutting concerns

Guideline: Pass authentication and database connections through context
Description: The context function runs on every request and provides shared resources to all resolvers. Use it to pass the authenticated user, database connections, and data loaders.
Do: Create a typed context with user, db, and loaders; authenticate in the context function; type the context
Dont: Do not access request headers in individual resolvers; do not create new db connections per resolver

Guideline: Use GraphQL input types for mutation arguments
Description: Define dedicated input types for mutations instead of passing individual scalar arguments. This improves readability, type safety, and makes it easier to add optional fields.
Do: Define input types for mutations; group related fields; make required fields non-nullable with !
Dont: Do not pass many individual arguments to mutations; do not reuse output types as input

Guideline: Implement custom scalars for DateTime, JSON, and domain-specific types
Description: Custom scalars handle serialization, parsing, and validation for types beyond the built-in String, Int, Float, Boolean, and ID. Use well-tested libraries for common scalars.
Do: Use graphql-scalars for common types like DateTime and JSON; implement custom scalars for domain-specific types
Dont: Do not represent dates as strings; do not skip validation in custom scalar parsers

Guideline: Use DataLoader to prevent N+1 query problems
Description: DataLoader batches and caches database lookups within a single request. Create per-request DataLoader instances to avoid stale data across requests while solving the N+1 problem.
Do: Create DataLoaders per request in context; batch by primary key; use caching within a single request
Dont: Do not create global DataLoaders that share cache across requests; do not query inside loops in resolvers

Guideline: Use PubSub with graphql-subscriptions for real-time updates
Description: GraphQL subscriptions enable real-time data via WebSockets. Use PubSub for in-memory events in development, and a distributed pub/sub (Redis) for production with multiple server instances.
Do: Use PubSub for dev; use Redis-backed PubSub for production; filter subscriptions to relevant data
Dont: Do not use in-memory PubSub in production with multiple instances; do not send excessive data over subscriptions

Guideline: Use GraphQLError with extensions for structured error responses
Description: Create custom error classes with error codes and use formatError to control what clients see. Include machine-readable codes for client-side error handling.
Do: Use GraphQLError with code extensions; format errors for production; log full errors server-side
Dont: Do not expose stack traces; do not use generic error messages without codes; do not throw plain Error objects

Guideline: Implement context-based authentication for GraphQL resolvers
Description: Extract and verify JWT tokens in the context function. Pass the authenticated user through context to all resolvers. Use helper functions to require authentication.
Do: Verify tokens in context; pass user through context; create requireAuth helper for protected resolvers
Dont: Do not verify tokens in individual resolvers; do not pass tokens through arguments; do not skip token expiration checks

Guideline: Implement field-level authorization with resolver guards
Description: Apply authorization checks at the field level to control what data each user can access. Use middleware patterns or directive-based auth for scalable permission management.
Do: Implement field-level checks; use role-based and ownership-based authorization; separate auth from business logic
Dont: Do not rely solely on query-level auth; do not expose sensitive fields to unauthorized users

Guideline: Implement cursor-based pagination with the Connection pattern
Description: Use the Relay Connection specification for consistent, efficient pagination. Define Edge and Connection types with cursors, pageInfo, and totalCount.
Do: Use cursor-based pagination with Connection/Edge types; include pageInfo with hasNextPage; encode cursors
Dont: Do not use offset-based pagination for large datasets; do not skip totalCount when clients need it

Guideline: Use fragments for reusable field selections
Description: Fragments define reusable sets of fields that can be included in multiple queries. Define them alongside your type definitions and use them in client-side operations for consistency.
Do: Define fragments for common field groups; use them in queries and mutations; colocate with components
Dont: Do not duplicate field selections across operations; do not create fragments that are too large

Guideline: Define GraphQL enums for constrained value sets
Description: Use GraphQL enum types for fields with a fixed set of values. Map them to TypeScript enums or string unions for type safety across the stack.
Do: Define enums in the schema for status fields, roles, and categories; map to TypeScript types
Dont: Do not use plain strings for constrained values; do not forget to handle all enum values in resolvers

Guideline: Use interfaces and union types for polymorphic data
Description: GraphQL interfaces define shared fields, and union types represent one of several possible types. Use __resolveType to tell GraphQL which concrete type to return.
Do: Define interfaces for shared fields; use unions for distinct types; always implement __resolveType
Dont: Do not create overly generic types with nullable fields instead of proper interfaces/unions

Guideline: Implement custom schema directives for cross-cutting concerns
Description: Custom directives add reusable behavior to schema fields, like authentication, caching, and input formatting. Use @mapperDirective with schema transformers.
Do: Use directives for cross-cutting concerns like auth, deprecation, and formatting; document directive usage
Dont: Do not overuse directives for business logic; do not create directives that duplicate resolver functionality

Guideline: Handle file uploads with graphql-upload middleware
Description: Use graphql-upload for handling multipart file uploads in GraphQL. Define an Upload scalar and process files in mutation resolvers with proper validation.
Do: Use graphql-upload middleware; validate file types and sizes; stream files to storage
Dont: Do not load entire files into memory; do not skip file type validation; do not store uploaded files in the public directory without scanning

Guideline: Use NestJS decorators for code-first GraphQL schema generation
Description: NestJS GraphQL module supports code-first schema generation using TypeScript decorators. Define types with @ObjectType, fields with @Field, and resolvers with @Resolver.
Do: Use decorators for type definitions; leverage class-validator for input validation; use NestJS DI for services
Dont: Do not mix code-first and schema-first approaches; do not skip validation decorators on inputs

Guideline: Load .graphql schema files and generate TypeScript types
Description: In the schema-first approach, define schemas in .graphql files and use graphql-codegen to generate TypeScript types for resolvers. Keep schemas as the single source of truth.
Do: Keep schemas in .graphql files; use graphql-codegen for types; organize by domain
Dont: Do not manually write resolver types; do not let types drift from schema; do not put all types in one file

Guideline: Use executeOperation for integration testing GraphQL resolvers
Description: Apollo Server's executeOperation method runs queries against your server without HTTP, making it fast and reliable for testing. Create a test server with mock context.
Do: Use executeOperation for resolver tests; mock context with auth and db; test both success and error paths
Dont: Do not test resolvers by calling them as plain functions; do not skip error case testing; do not use real databases in unit tests

Guideline: Combine multiple GraphQL schemas with schema stitching or federation
Description: Use @graphql-tools/stitch to combine multiple schemas into a single gateway. Define type merging for shared types and delegate fields between schemas.
Do: Use stitchSchemas with type merging; keep subschemas independent; define clear merge boundaries
Dont: Do not duplicate types across schemas without merging; do not create circular dependencies between subschemas

Guideline: Implement query complexity and rate limiting for GraphQL APIs
Description: GraphQL APIs are vulnerable to expensive queries. Use query complexity analysis and rate limiting to prevent abuse and protect server resources.
Do: Use complexity analysis to limit query depth and cost; implement per-user rate limits; reject overly complex queries
Dont: Do not allow unbounded query depth; do not skip rate limiting; do not treat all queries as equal cost

Guideline: Configure Apollo cache control for response-level and field-level caching
Description: Use cacheControl hints on schema fields to enable CDN and client caching. Set maxAge and scope (PUBLIC or PRIVATE) to control caching behavior per field.
Do: Set cache hints on fields; use PUBLIC for shared data and PRIVATE for user-specific data; configure CDN headers
Dont: Do not cache user-specific data as PUBLIC; do not set maxAge too high for frequently changing data; do not skip caching entirely

Guideline: Use graphql-codegen to generate TypeScript types from schema and operations
Description: graphql-codegen generates TypeScript types from your GraphQL schema and client operations. This ensures end-to-end type safety between schema, resolvers, and client queries.
Do: Configure codegen for both server resolvers and client operations; run codegen in CI; use typed document nodes
Dont: Do not manually write GraphQL types; do not skip codegen in CI; do not import from generated files without running codegen first
